subsystem: A Chromium base::CommandLine argument denylist filter. IsBlacklistedArg() takes a single command-line argument, normalizes it (Windows: wide->UTF-8), strips a leading switch prefix ("--", "-", "/"), extracts the switch NAME up to '=', and binary-searches a sorted constant table kBlacklist. It returns true when the switch is on the blacklist. Its role is a security guard: it decides whether a switch is forbidden so a caller can strip or reject it before it reaches a more-trusted process. The sample tag /* sample: primevul_train_2097_vuln */ marks it as a PrimeVul training sample.
provenance: Identified from Chromium //base idioms: the parameter type base::CommandLine::CharType (line 2), base::WideToUTF8 (line 4), base::StartsWith with base::CompareCase::SENSITIVE (line 14), and the OS_WIN preprocessor guard (line 3). The kBlacklist symbol searched with std::lower_bound (lines 23-24) is a Chromium-style sorted static switch table. The leading comment /* sample: primevul_train_2097_vuln */ (line 1) identifies the PrimeVul dataset origin.
trust_boundary: The single input arg is an attacker-influenced command-line token. In Chromium this pattern is used to sanitize switches that flow across a privilege boundary (e.g. a launcher, a less-trusted process, or externally-supplied command lines building the argv of a more-trusted child process). The entire purpose of the function is to be a protection mechanism, so arg and its decomposed switch_name are hostile data whose only safe outcome is: any switch that the downstream consumer would honor as a blacklisted switch MUST be classified as blacklisted here. Every place where this function's notion of a 'switch name' can diverge from the real command-line parser's notion is a filter-bypass surface.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point. It receives a pointer to one command-line argument in the platform's native character type (base::CommandLine::CharType is wchar_t on Windows, char on POSIX) and returns a bool indicating whether that argument names a blacklisted switch. Because it is a security predicate, a false return means 'allow this switch', so any false-negative is a filter bypass, and any crash on hostile input is a denial-of-service in the sanitizing process.

Platform normalization. On Windows the native wide argument is converted to a UTF-8 std::string named converted, and a is set to point into that string's buffer via c_str(). On POSIX arg is already char* so a aliases it directly. Note that converted is a plain function-scope local (the #if/#else/#endif are preprocessor directives, not a C++ block scope), so converted lives until the function returns and a remains valid for every later use — this is NOT a dangling-c_str() bug. From here on a is a NUL-terminated byte string that the rest of the function treats as the switch text.

The set of switch-introducer prefixes the function recognizes: GNU-style "--", single-dash "-", and Windows-style "/". The array is ordered longest-first so that "--" is tested before "-"; this ordering matters because "--foo" also starts with "-", and matching the shorter prefix first would leave a stray '-' in the switch name. static means it is built once.

Prefix detection. prefix_length starts at 0. The loop walks the three prefixes in order and, on the first case-SENSITIVE StartsWith match, records the matched prefix's length via strlen and breaks. If arg carries no recognized prefix (e.g. a bare positional argument) prefix_length stays 0. The case-sensitivity choice (base::CompareCase::SENSITIVE) is the first place the filter fixes an interpretation of the input that must match the downstream parser exactly.

Switch-name extraction. Only when a prefix was found does the function proceed: a is advanced past the prefix bytes, then switch_name is constructed from the first strcspn(a, "=") characters — i.e. everything from the current position up to (but not including) the first '=' or the terminating NUL. This defines the function's notion of a switch name: prefix stripped, value (after '=') stripped. This is bounded and safe: strcspn on a NUL-terminated string returns at most the string length, so the std::string(ptr, count) construction never reads past the buffer.

The lookup. std::lower_bound performs a binary search over the constant table kBlacklist for switch_name using the default operator< (byte-wise, case-sensitive std::string comparison). It returns the first element not less than switch_name; the code then confirms the element exists (iter != end) and is an exact match (switch_name == *iter) before returning true. Binary search is only correct if kBlacklist is sorted under the very same comparator, and the equality test is only meaningful if it uses the same case/normalization rules the downstream consumer uses.

The default/allow path. Reached when there was no prefix, when the switch name is not in the blacklist, or when the binary search found a near-but-not-equal entry. Because this is the permissive result of a security filter, everything that reaches here is treated as an allowed switch by the caller.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Case-sensitive matching (reinforced by the default case-sensitive std::string compare in lower_bound at lines 23-25) makes the denylist bypassable if the downstream command-line consumer treats switch names case-insensitively. On Windows, Chromium historically lower-cases ASCII switch names during command-line parsing and Windows switch/path handling is broadly case-insensitive; an attacker who wants a blacklisted switch such as "foo" can instead pass "--Foo" or "--FOO". This function computes switch_name="Foo", which is byte-unequal to the lower-cased table entry "foo", so IsBlacklistedArg returns false and the caller forwards the switch — yet the real parser normalizes it back to "foo" and honors it. The blocked capability (often a logging/diagnostic switch) then takes effect, i.e. an information-exposure or protection-mechanism failure. Invariant: the filter's case-folding must be identical to the consumer's.

std::lower_bound is a binary search whose result is defined only if the range [begin(kBlacklist), end(kBlacklist)) is sorted with respect to the same operator< used for the search. kBlacklist is not shown, so this is a latent contract in the implementation: if the table is edited out of order, contains a mixed-case or differently-normalized entry, or is sorted with a different comparator (e.g. a locale/CI collation), lower_bound can step past a present blacklisted entry and return end() or a wrong neighbor. The exact-match guard (line 25) then fails and the function returns false for a switch that IS on the list — a silent denylist bypass. Invariant: kBlacklist must be strictly sorted under byte-wise operator< matching the comparison here, and this should be asserted at build time.

Interpretation conflict between this filter's prefix model and the real parser. The function recognizes exactly "--", "-", and "/" and strips only ONE prefix. If the downstream parser accepts a prefix form this list omits, or reduces multiple leading dashes differently, the switch_name computed here diverges from the honored switch name. For example "---foo" matches "--" here (line 14), yielding switch_name="-foo" (line 22) which will not equal the table entry "foo", while a consumer that strips all leading dashes sees "foo". Similarly, if a value uses a separator other than '=' (a space-delimited value or a ':'), strcspn(a,"=") over- or under-captures the name. Any such mismatch is a filter bypass. Invariant: the prefix set, the number of stripped prefix characters, and the name/value delimiter must exactly mirror base::CommandLine's own parsing.

On Windows the check runs on a UTF-8 transcoding of the native wide argument, but the actual command line is dispatched from the wide form. WideToUTF8 can be lossy or normalizing for ill-formed UTF-16 (unpaired surrogates -> U+FFFD) and does no Unicode case-folding or homoglyph normalization. An attacker can craft a wide argument whose UTF-8 form fails the byte comparison here yet is still interpreted as a blacklisted switch by the wide-string parser downstream, again bypassing the denylist. Invariant: normalization applied before the blacklist comparison must be identical to whatever the real switch matcher applies to the wide string.

Null / malformed input robustness. arg is dereferenced with no NULL check: base::WideToUTF8(arg) on Windows (line 4) and base::StartsWith(a, prefix, ...) on all platforms (line 14) both read through a. If any caller can pass a NULL or non-NUL-terminated argument, this crashes the sanitizing process (NULL-pointer dereference / out-of-bounds read), a denial-of-service. Invariant: callers must guarantee arg is a valid NUL-terminated string, or the function must guard against NULL.

summary: IsBlacklistedArg is a command-line switch denylist guard, not a memory-safety hazard: the buffer handling (prefix strip, strcspn-bounded std::string, function-scoped c_str) is safe. The real risk is protection-mechanism failure — the check matches switch names case-sensitively (lines 14, 23-25), depends on an unshown kBlacklist being correctly sorted, and can diverge from the real parser on prefix handling and Windows wide->UTF-8 normalization. Any such divergence lets an attacker slip a blacklisted (e.g. information-exposing) switch through, so the top exposure is a filter bypass rather than an overflow.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Security predicate over attacker-influenced input; arg is dereferenced without a NULL guard.
Windows-only transcoding can normalize/lose bytes so the compared string diverges from what the wide parser honors.
Not a dangling-pointer UAF: converted is a function-scope local (the #if is preprocessor, not a block), so it outlives every use of a.
Direct alias of the caller's buffer on POSIX; safe as long as arg is valid, covered by the NULL-input finding.
The recognized prefix set and single-prefix strip must mirror the real parser or the extracted switch name diverges (filter bypass).
Case-sensitive prefix/name match enables case-variant bypass if the consumer is case-insensitive.
Correct: strlen of a literal prefix that already matched via StartsWith; advancing a by it stays within the string.
Bounded: only executed when a prefix matched, so a advances at most to the NUL; no over-read.
Splitting only on '=' can mis-tokenize versus the consumer's name/value rule; the construction itself is bounds-safe.
Binary search requires kBlacklist to be sorted under the same comparator; unsorted/mis-normalized table silently misses entries.
The exact-match/end guard is itself correct; its weakness (case sensitivity) is captured by the lines 14/23-25 finding.
Unshown denylist contents/order are the trust anchor; sortedness and normalization of its entries are latent contracts.
Plain boolean results; return false is the permissive path whose risk is fully attributable to the matching-logic findings above.

findings.title: Case-sensitive denylist match allows switch-filter bypass
findings.anchor: base::StartsWith(a, prefix, base::CompareCase::SENSITIVE) and default case-sensitive std::string compare in lower_bound / ==
findings.bug_class: Case-sensitivity blacklist bypass (protection-mechanism failure, CWE-693) enabling information exposure (CWE-200)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0030_cwe200.cpp (matching logic) plus the command-line parser in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: The case-folding/normalization used to match switch names here must be byte-for-byte identical to what the downstream command-line parser uses when it decides to honor a switch.
findings.failure_mode: If the consumer treats switch names case-insensitively (Chromium lower-cases ASCII switch names on Windows; Windows switch/path handling is broadly case-insensitive), an attacker passes a case variant such as "--Foo" of a blacklisted "foo". switch_name becomes "Foo", byte-unequal to the table entry, so the function returns false and the caller forwards the switch, which the parser normalizes back to "foo" and honors — re-enabling a blocked (often diagnostic/information-exposing) capability.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the real switch parser lower-cases or otherwise case-folds switch names (and whether kBlacklist entries are stored lower-cased), i.e. whether SENSITIVE matching here can diverge from the consumer.

findings.title: Binary search depends on unshown kBlacklist being correctly sorted
findings.bug_class: Incorrect comparison / broken binary-search precondition -> silent filter bypass
findings.where_it_lives: kBlacklist definition in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: kBlacklist must be strictly sorted under exactly the operator< used by lower_bound (byte-wise std::string ordering), and this should be enforced by a static/DCHECK assertion.
findings.failure_mode: std::lower_bound has undefined results on an unsorted range. If kBlacklist is edited out of order, or an entry is stored with different case/normalization than the search key, lower_bound can skip a present blacklisted entry and return end() or a wrong neighbor; the equality guard then fails and IsBlacklistedArg returns false for a switch that is on the list — a silent denylist bypass with no crash.
findings.what_to_confirm: That kBlacklist is sorted with the same comparator used here and that a build-time/DCHECK check guarantees it stays sorted.

findings.title: Prefix and value-delimiter model may diverge from the real parser
findings.anchor: prefixes[] = {"--", "-", "/"} with single-prefix strip and strcspn(a, "=")
findings.bug_class: Interpretation conflict between filter and consumer (switch tokenization mismatch)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0030_cwe200.cpp plus the command-line parser in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: The recognized prefix set, the number of leading prefix characters stripped, and the name/value delimiter must exactly mirror how base::CommandLine tokenizes a switch into name and value.
findings.failure_mode: The filter strips exactly one of "--"/"-"/"/" and splits the name only at '='. Inputs like "---foo" (matches "--" -> switch_name "-foo") or a value delimited by something other than '=' produce a switch_name that differs from the name the consumer ultimately honors, so a blacklisted switch is not recognized and passes through.
findings.what_to_confirm: Enumerate every prefix form and value delimiter base::CommandLine accepts and confirm this normalization reproduces the consumer's switch name exactly.

findings.title: Windows wide->UTF-8 transcoding can diverge from the wide parser
findings.code:
const auto converted = base::WideToUTF8(arg);
  const char* a = converted.c_str();
findings.bug_class: Improper handling of Unicode / normalization mismatch -> filter bypass
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0030_cwe200.cpp (Windows path) plus the wide command-line parser in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: Any transcoding/normalization applied before the blacklist comparison must be identical to what the actual (wide-string) switch matcher applies.
findings.failure_mode: The check compares a UTF-8 transcoding while the command is dispatched from the wide form. WideToUTF8 substitutes replacement characters for ill-formed UTF-16 and performs no case-folding or homoglyph normalization, so an attacker can craft a wide argument whose UTF-8 bytes miss the blacklist entry yet is still parsed as the blacklisted switch downstream.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the downstream matcher operates on the wide string and how it normalizes it, versus the UTF-8 comparison performed here.

findings.title: No NULL / termination check on arg
findings.anchor: IsBlacklistedArg(const base::CommandLine::CharType* arg) dereferenced via WideToUTF8 / StartsWith
findings.bug_class: NULL-pointer dereference / out-of-bounds read on hostile input
findings.invariant: Callers must pass a valid NUL-terminated argument, or the function must reject NULL before dereferencing.
findings.failure_mode: If any caller can pass NULL or a non-NUL-terminated buffer, WideToUTF8(arg) (line 4) or StartsWith(a, ...) (line 14) dereferences it and crashes the sanitizing process (DoS) or reads out of bounds.
findings.what_to_confirm: Every call site guarantees arg is a valid NUL-terminated string, or add a NULL guard at entry.

audit_checklist: Confirm whether the downstream command-line parser matches switch names case-insensitively (esp. on Windows) while this filter matches case-sensitively (lines 14, 23-25) — the primary bypass.
Verify kBlacklist is strictly sorted under the same operator< used by std::lower_bound and that a static/DCHECK assertion enforces it (lines 23-25).
Confirm kBlacklist entries are stored in the exact normalized form (case, prefix-stripped) that switch_name is computed in.
Enumerate every switch prefix and name/value delimiter base::CommandLine accepts and prove this single-prefix + strcspn(=) normalization reproduces the honored switch name (lines 10-22).
On Windows, check that comparing a WideToUTF8 form (line 4) cannot diverge from how the wide command line is parsed (surrogates, case, Unicode normalization).
Ensure every caller passes a valid NUL-terminated arg, or add a NULL guard before the dereferences at lines 4 and 14.
